title: Memorials Of A Tour In Italy, 1837 - VIII. - Near Rome, In Sight Of St. Peter's
content:
Long has the dew been dried on tree and lawn:
 O'er man and beast a not unwelcome boon
 Is shed, the languor of approaching noon;
 To shady rest withdrawing or withdrawn
 Mute are all creatures, as this couchant fawn,
 Save insect-swarms that hum in air afloat,
 Save that the Cock is crowing, a shrill note,
 Startling and shrill as that which roused the dawn.
 Heard in that hour, or when, as now, the nerve
 Shrinks from the note as from a mistimed thing,
 Oft for a holy warning may it serve,
 Charged with remembrance of 'his' sudden sting,
 His bitter tears, whose name the Papal Chair
 And yon resplendent Church are proud to bear.
meaning:
About: William Wordsworth (April 7, 1770 � April 23, 1850) was a major English romantic poet who, with Samuel Taylor Coleridge, helped launch the Romantic Age in English literature with their 1798 joint publication, Lyrical Ballads.
 Wordsworth's masterpiece is generally considered to be The Prelude, an autobiographical poem of his early years that was revised and expanded a number of times. It was never published during his lifetime, and was only given the title after his death. Up until this time it was generally known as the poem "to Coleridge". 
Wordsworth was England's Poet Laureate from 1843 until his death in 1850.
